title: Model shortlist showcase + bench matrix walkthrough — Sable Tech
preview:
Meeting Header:
Date: 2025-04-30
Time: 15:00 UTC
Duration: 48 minutes
Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Rosa Alvarez (Sable Tech - Head of ML), Tom Becker (Sable Tech - Platform), Priya Nair (Sable Tech - CTO)

Auto-summary (auto-generated, imperfect):
- Quick demo of model catalog shortlist and bench matrix; discussed latency vs cost tradeoffs and private deployment constraints.
- Agreed to share bench spreadsheet and run a short POC focusing on 2 model variants and an optimized quant profile.
- Action: Redwood to send recommended config and cost estimation; Sable to confirm residency and POC users.

Topics: model shortlist overview; benchmark interpretation; compatibility matrix (GPU/kernel mapping); private vs hosted tradeoffs; POC scope

--- Transcript ---
[00:00] Maya Chen: Okay everyone, thanks for jumping on. I think we have about 45 minutes, so we'll walk through a focused shortlist showcase from our catalog and then the bench matrix that Ethan prepared. Uh, quick intros?
[00:08] Rosa Alvarez: Hi, Rosa here, head of ML at Sable. We're looking to replace a homegrown answer-generation that is getting expensive and slow at scale.
[00:13] Tom Becker: Tom, platform, I'll be the ops contact.
[00:16] Priya Nair: Priya, CTO — I'll listen for security and residency implications.
[00:20] Ethan Park: Ethan, SE at Redwood, I'll be taking you through benchmarks and compat matrix.
[00:24] Maya Chen: Great. Quick agenda: shortlist rationale, bench spreadsheet walkthrough, and then recommended runtime configs — hosted/dedicated/private implications. We'll leave 10 minutes for Q&A and next steps.

[01:02] Ethan Park: Alright, so first off a note about the shortlist. We filtered open models that fit Sable's latency target (~150ms p50) and token cost goals. We then added a small bench on a realistic prompt set from your notes. One caveat — the first pass uses 2-k sequence assumptions that may not be exact for your usage.
[01:28] Rosa Alvarez: Right, most of our calls are ~400 to 800 tokens. So please keep that in mind.
[01:33] Ethan Park: Yup, we truncated the bench to 512 and 1024 runs for comparability.

[02:05] Ethan Park: (screen sharing) Here's the bench matrix — columns are model, hardware used (A100, H100, and a 4-bit quantized variant on T4 for edge tests, note: the T4 runs are emulated), p50/p95 latency, cost per 1k tokens, and qualitative output score (we used your prompt rubric).
[02:28] Tom Becker: Can you clarify the cost column — is that Redwood hosted pricing or infra cost?
[02:33] Maya Chen: Good q, Tom. The cost is normalized infra cost assumptions we used for benching to give apples-to-apples; your actual hosted pricing would include our unit markup. We'll send both numbers in the spreadsheet.
[02:45] Rosa Alvarez: I see a model that looks promising but the name's a bit off in the transcript — it says "Mistral-mini" but we actually ran the bench on Mistral-Instruct-7b variant, right?
[02:56] Ethan Park: Yeah sorry the label got shortened; it is the 7B instruct style we used. Transcription did that weird truncation in the sheet too.

[03:20] Ethan Park: Key observation: Model A (7B instruct) at 4-bit quant gives near the same p50 but costs ~30% less vs FP16 on H100 for your prompt mix. Model B (13B) gives better quality on the long-form rubric but p95 latency jumps and cost increases materially.
[03:45] Priya Nair: When you say "4-bit quant" do you mean post-training quantization or a quant-aware kernel? We need to know for compliance and reproducibility.
[03:53] Ethan Park: Good call. This bench used a quant-aware runtime path — KV cache intact, static quant profiles. It's not an off-the-shelf post-train quant that loses reproducibility; it's our compilation+kernel selection that maps to a quantized execution. We'll include the exact flags in the bench artifact.
[04:12] Rosa Alvarez: Do you have any notes about deterministic outputs? Our use-case requires high consistency for audit logs.
[04:18] Maya Chen: We can pin randomness and provide seed controls; also Redwood's private deployment can disable ephemeral caching across requests if you need strict determinism, but that affects throughput.
[04:27] Tom Becker: That tradeoff is important. We need to see throughput numbers when KV caching is disabled.
[04:32] Ethan Park: Noted, we'll add a KV-disabled run to the next bench pass.

[05:00] Ethan Park: Now on compatibility matrix — this is where we map model architecture to kernel families and hardware. For example, the 7B instruct aligns well with our fused attention kernels on A100 and H100; on T4 it's more limited and we fallback to a slower kernel which is why the emulated T4 runs look worse.
[05:22] Rosa Alvarez: Is there an easy way in the Console to see which kernels are selected?
[05:26] Maya Chen: Yes, in Redwood Console under model -> runtime details you'll see kernel mappings, kernel versions, and perf profiles per sequence length. We'll share a short GIF showing that.
[05:34] Priya Nair: For private deployment — will we need specific AMIs or a docker image? How do we manage KMS integration?
[05:40] Ethan Park: Private ships as a customer-managed control plane with our agent. We provide a hardened container image plus an ops guide for KMS/HSM integrations. KMS calls remain in-customer VPC. We'll attach that doc.
[05:51] Maya Chen: We also have an on-prem package if you need air-gapped installs; that's common with finance customers.
[05:58] Priya Nair: Good. Data residency — our data must remain in EU for certain customers. Do you support region pinning for model hosting?
[06:06] Maya Chen: Yes, region pinning and routing policies can be set per-route or per-customer. For PII workflows we recommend Private with VPC egress rules.

[06:35] Rosa Alvarez: Back to bench interpretation — the quality metric looks like a single score; can we get the underlying prompt-level ratings?
[06:41] Ethan Park: Absolutely, the sheet includes prompt-level rows and rater notes. We used a mix of automated evals and human A/B checks for edge cases.
[06:50] Tom Becker: On the subject of latency vs cost, what about batching? Our usage pattern has many short bursts with concurrency — batching might actually add latency.
[06:58] Ethan Park: Correct. We included continuous batching profiles. For your bursty pattern we recommend smaller batch sizes and aggressive prefix caching; that preserves p50 while keeping p95 acceptable. We'll show config suggestions.

[07:30] Rosa Alvarez: Any callouts about fallbacks? We don't want user-facing outages if a model variant is overloaded.
[07:36] Maya Chen: Redwood supports policy-based fallbacks: route to cheaper model variant or fallback to a smaller local cached answer with a disclaimer. You can set canary and A/B rollouts in Console.
[07:45] Priya Nair: Ok, who owns SLA for a private deployment?
[07:49] Maya Chen: For Private we offer a support tier; SLA is part of the enterprise contract. Dedicated pools have SLA baked in.

[08:20] Ethan Park: (scrolling) I want to highlight two rows we flagged for Sable. Row 3: 7B-4q with A100 — lowest cost and meets p50. Row 7: 13B-FP16 on H100 — best quality but cost ~2.6x and p95 spikes.
[08:39] Rosa Alvarez: So recommend the 7B quant for most real-time flows and reserve 13B for async tasks?
[08:44] Ethan Park: Exactly, that's the classic split.

[09:00] Tom Becker: A couple operational Qs — what's the rollout cadence if we want to swap a model variant in prod? Is there a safer canary?
[09:07] Maya Chen: Console supports canary traffic splits; you can route a % to new variant, monitor per-route cost/latency, and auto-roll back on regressions. We also provide hooks for custom eval sets to gate rollouts.

[09:35] Rosa Alvarez: Competitor question: we've looked at Anthropic's options and Vertex. How does Redwood's catalog help versus just using managed endpoints?
[09:42] Maya Chen: Two main differences — unified dev experience across hosted/dedicated/private deployments, and deeper runtime optimizations/kernels that lower unit cost. We also surface per-model kernel compatibility and recommended quant profiles which typical managed endpoints don't expose.
[09:58] Priya Nair: Also curious about logging — do you capture token-level latency for tracing?
[10:04] Ethan Park: Yes, we support token-level traces, top-down latency breakdowns, and token counts per request; you can export traces to your observability tool.

[10:30] (Silence while Ethan scrolls)
[10:55] Ethan Park: Alright the next slide is an example POC design: two-week POC, ingest 10k representative prompts, run A/B across 7B-4q and 13B-FP16, measure p50/p95, quality delta, and cost per 1M tokens.
[11:12] Rosa Alvarez: That sounds fine. Who provisions the infra for the POC?
[11:16] Maya Chen: For hosted POC it's on us; for Private POC we collaborate — you provide the cluster and we provide the deployment artifacts.
[11:24] Tom Becker: We probably want to start hosted to iterate quickly then move to Private.
[11:29] Priya Nair: Agree. Security team will require SOC2 docs and a quick network diagram.
[11:34] Maya Chen: We'll include the SOC2 report and a network diagram template in the follow-ups.

[12:05] Rosa Alvarez: A random nit — the bench spreadsheet shows some outliers at p95 for 7B-4q; can you explain if those are garbage collection pauses or kernel cold starts?
[12:13] Ethan Park: Those were warmup effects and a couple of small NVidia kernel scheduling glitches during the run. We'll re-run with longer warmups and also add kernel warm pools to smooth p95.
[12:26] Tom Becker: Good. Also, can we see a breakdown of which prompts cause most latency?
[12:30] Ethan Park: Yes, the prompt-level sheet includes that and we'll add a tag column for heavy-attention patterns.

[13:00] (Several sections skipped in auto-transcript)
[18:40] Maya Chen: Quick demo of Console: here's where you filter the catalog by model size, latency profile, and whether it has verified kernel support for H100. You can also pin sequence-length buckets to see perf differences across lengths.
[19:03] Rosa Alvarez: The UI looks neat. Is there an API for that?
[19:06] Ethan Park: Yep, Catalog API returns perf profiles and compatibility flags. We'll include curl examples.

[20:20] Priya Nair: On compliance — encryption at rest and in transit?
[20:24] Maya Chen: We support TLS in transit and customer-managed KMS for at-rest keys for Private. For Hosted we offer envelope encryption with customer keys in many cases.

[21:00] Tom Becker: Anything specific about model provenance? We need to track which open weights were used and any license obligations.
[21:07] Ethan Park: Catalog includes provenance metadata: model source, license, commit hash, and vetting notes. We also record the runtime artifact version used for inference so you can reproduce results.

[23:10] Rosa Alvarez: Can we lock a model version so future runs don't get silently upgraded?
[23:14] Maya Chen: Yes, you can pin a model version in Console or via API. Rolling updates require an explicit rollout action.

[25:00] Ethan Park: One more bench nuance — quant profiles vary depending on activation distribution. We provided both generic 4-bit and an optimized per-model profile we tune during onboarding. The optimized profile shaved another 8-12% off cost in our internal runs.
[25:22] Rosa Alvarez: How invasive is the tuning? Does it require retraining?
[25:25] Ethan Park: No retraining — it's a calibration pass (like PTQ calibration) plus kernel selection tweaks. It runs on a small corpus and produces a profile. We'll include the workflow.

[28:40] Priya Nair: What's the expected timeline to get to production after POC?
[28:45] Maya Chen: Typically 6–10 weeks including security review and low-impact migration for medium complexity customers. If you want fast, hosted route can be a couple weeks.

[30:02] Rosa Alvarez: Budget question: do you have an estimate for monthly spend at 20M tokens?
[30:09] Ethan Park: Rough estimate in the sheet: with 7B-4q on reserved dedicated pools it's X per 1M tokens — we'll replace X with concrete numbers in the artifact. For 20M tokens multiply accordingly — we'll send an itemized cost table.

[31:10] Tom Becker: SLOs — can we set per-route latency SLOs?
[31:15] Maya Chen: Yes, Console supports per-route SLO targets and will auto-scale Dedicated pools within policy.

[33:40] (Long form Q&A, some crosstalk, occasional filler 'uh', 'okay')
[38:00] Rosa Alvarez: Ok this has been super helpful. Quick recap of next steps?
[38:05] Maya Chen: Recap: Maya sends bench spreadsheet and raw logs; Ethan sends the recommended config and a one-pager; we schedule POC kickoff tentatively week of 2025-05-12; Sable confirms POC users and residency constraints.
[38:18] Priya Nair: We'll confirm POC participants by 2025-05-05.

[39:02] Tom Becker: A small ask — can you highlight kernel mappings for the 7B-4q row in the sheet? Makes handoffs easier.
[39:09] Ethan Park: Yep will do.

[40:00] (Wrap up)
[40:10] Maya Chen: Thanks everyone. We'll follow up with the artifacts and proposed POC plan.
[40:15] Rosa Alvarez: Thanks, bye.

Auto-Action Items (reiterated):
- Maya: send bench spreadsheet, raw logs, SOC2 and network diagram template (due 2025-05-02)
- Ethan: recommended model config, kernel map highlight, KV-disabled bench pass (due 2025-05-06)
- Rosa: confirm POC participants and residency constraints (due 2025-05-05)

End of transcript.

References/Links mentioned:
- Bench spreadsheet (link provided in chat)
- Redwood Console model runtime GIF (link to console demo)
- SOC2 report and Private deployment network diagram template (to be attached)

Notes on transcription: speaker times approximate; some product names truncated in auto labels (e.g., 'Mistral' variants).

title: Crimson Health vendor risk questionnaire triage & evidence roadmap
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-04-30
Start time: 15:00 UTC
Duration: ~58 minutes
Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Sofia Alvarez (Redwood CSM), Aisha Gomez (Crimson Head of IT Risk), Liam O'Connor (Crimson Security Architect), Priya Rao (Crimson Procurement), Jonah Matthews (Crimson DevOps)

Summary:
High-level walk-through of Crimson's vendor security questionnaire (SIG + vendor-specific controls). Focus areas: SOC 2 evidence, KMS/customer-managed keys, VPC/private deployment options, audit log forwarding and retention, data residency EU vs. global, RBAC/SSO provisioning (SAML), and evidence delivery plan. Agreed to share Redwood CAIQ baseline + SOC 2 Type II within one week and schedule a technical KMS deep-dive.

Topics covered:
- Evidence bundle: SOC 2 Type II, penetration test summary, CAIQ/SIG answers, architecture diagram, RBAC/SAML screenshots, audit log export examples
- Encryption: at-rest + in-transit, customer-managed KMS (BYOK) options, HSM mention
- Network: Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private (VPC) tradeoffs, private control plane, egress restrictions
- Logging & SIEM: syslog/CloudWatch export, audit trails, retention policies and SLA for log retrieval
- Compliance: timeline for evidence delivery, attestation language, subprocessor list

Action items recap (captured above) and next meeting to focus on KMS integration details and any outstanding SIG clarifications.

Transcript body:
[00:00] Maya Chen: Hey everyone, thanks for joining, um we're — I'm Maya, AE on the account and with me is Ethan our security SE and Sofia from customer success.
[00:07] Aisha Gomez: Hi Maya, hi Ethan, thanks for having us. We wanted to triage the Crimson vendor form first — there's a lot in the SIG and then we have a handful of additional local controls.
[00:18] Ethan Park: Great, yep. Our plan is to go section by section, flag the asks that need evidence, and decide which ones are satisfied by SOC 2 artifacts versus additional screenshots or architecture notes.
[00:27] [00:30] Priya Rao: Quick ask from procurement — we need to know timeline for the SOC 2 report and whether Redwood supports customer-managed keys. That's a blocker for our legal review.
[00:39] Maya Chen: Understood. We have a SOC 2 Type II report covering the Redwood hosted API and Dedicated offerings — we'll share the redacted PDF. For KMS, we do support customer-managed keys for Private deployments and we have integrations with major cloud KMS providers and HSM-backed keys. For hosted customers we offer a hosted KMS option and we can discuss BYOK on a case-by-case basis.
[01:05] Liam O'Connor: Can you clarify hosted KMS = Redwood holds the key material? And for Private, the customer controls the KMS? The wording in our form is pretty specific about BYOK and HSM attestation (FIPS 140-2/3).
[01:20] Ethan Park: Yep — that's the general split. Hosted API defaults to Redwood-managed encryption keys (we use AES-256 at rest and TLS 1.3 in transit), but for Private(VPC/on-prem) you can bring your own key via Cloud KMS or an HSM. We can provide attestation for HSM usage, and if FIPS certification evidence is required we can surface the vendor's HSM cert.
[01:41] [01:45] Jonah Matthews: Related — how do you handle access to KV caches and ephemeral data? Our concern is PHI staying in memory or in logs.
[01:53] Ethan Park: Good question. KV cache is ephemeral and lifecycle-managed; we purge session-level caches after the request completes unless cached explicitly in configured cache buckets. By default we retain minimal request metadata for debugging (trace IDs, error codes) and full request/response bodies are not stored in hosted mode unless the customer elects to enable retention for training — which Crimson explicitly won't.
[02:16] Aisha Gomez: Can you point us to the data residency options? We need EU-only for EU patients, and Canada for a subset of services.
[02:24] Maya Chen: Yes — we offer region pinning for Hosted with EU (Frankfurt) and UK regions and we have Dedicated/Private deployments that can be deployed in customer-region VPCs — for on-prem we can run the Private control plane entirely in your network. We'll add a clear note in the evidence package about which models/reservations are available in each region.
[02:46] [03:00] Priya Rao: About audit logs — can Redwood forward logs to our SIEM? We need immutable audit trails for 12 months and the ability to export on demand.
[03:09] Ethan Park: We support streaming audit logs to customer endpoints: S3 exports, CloudWatch, or syslog collectors. Retention is configurable in Dedicated/Private; in Hosted there's a standard retention window but we can discuss extended retention as part of an enterprise add-on. We also provide queryable dashboards in the Console for 90 days by default.
[03:33] Liam O'Connor: Do you have examples of the audit events? We need user provisioning events, key rotation, model access, admin actions, and API call metadata to match our control mapping.
[03:44] Ethan Park: Yes, we'll include a sample audit extract showing event types and fields — who, what, when, source IP, request id, model id, token usage.
[03:54] [04:00] Aisha Gomez: On RBAC and SSO — we require SCIM provisioning and SAML 2.0 SSO. Is that in scope?
[04:08] Maya Chen: We support SAML 2.0 SSO and SCIM-based provisioning for RBAC in the Console. Role mapping is configurable; we can show screenshots of groups and assigned roles. We'll also provide the Admin API docs for automated provisioning.
[04:21] Priya Rao: A note on subprocessors — do you publish your list? Our legal wants to know any third parties that have access to customer data.
[04:29] Sofia Alvarez: Yes — we maintain a subprocessor list and update it quarterly. We'll include the current list with datacenter regions and the nature of access.
[04:37] [05:00] Jonah Matthews: About vulnerability management and pen testing — is there a pentest summary and remediation timeline?
[05:07] Ethan Park: We do annual third-party pen tests and regular internal scans. The pen test summary and our high-level remediation process will be part of the SOC 2 and evidence bundle. We can redact specifics but provide enough to satisfy vendor assessors.
[05:24] Liam O'Connor: For signature/evidence, we prefer a single folder (SFTP or secure share) with: SOC 2 pdf, CAIQ spreadsheet, network diagram, screenshots for RBAC/SAML, sample logs, and pen test summary. Is that doable?

[09:33] Ethan Park: We'll provide a crypto summary: AES-256-GCM at rest, KMS key wrapping via RSA-OAEP or equivalent depending on provider, and TLS 1.2/1.3 with modern cipher suites. For HSMs we can call out FIPS validations.

[10:38] Sofia Alvarez: We have a legal operations process and an assigned response team. Typical turnaround for a preservation/initial response is 48–72 business hours, and we'll include the process summary in the bundle.
[10:53] [11:00] Jonah Matthews: For the evidence delivery, can we have a single point of contact at Redwood? We prefer communications go via that person to avoid distribution errors.
[11:08] Maya Chen: Yes, I'll be the primary contact and Sofia will assist with follow ups.

[11:24] Maya Chen: Correct. Dedicated is reserved capacity with monthly/annual commitment; Private has a platform fee and support contract. I'll send a high-level pricing note but pricing details to be covered with Sales Ops in the commercial thread.
[11:42] [12:00] Aisha Gomez: Any outstanding items we should track?
[12:07] Ethan Park: We'll need to confirm exact EU/Canada region list from you to scope evidence and subprocessor mapping, and we'll prepare the CAIQ detailed answers with references to doc pages.

We'll schedule the technical KMS deep-dive next week after you review the initial bundle.
[12:45] Aisha Gomez: Sounds good, thanks everyone.
[12:48] Maya Chen: Thanks all — we'll follow up with the secure link and the SIG mapping.
[12:52] [13:00] Meeting ends.

title: Go/No-Go: Dedicated staged rollouts (beta) + incident review + launch checklist
preview: Internal go/no-go for enabling Dedicated staged rollouts for a limited beta (1–2 design partners). Reviewed early beta failures (429 spike due to canary partition starving effective capacity, gate evidence missing due to telemetry tag mismatch, and metrics cardinality regression). Aligning on ship criteria: feature flag default OFF, validated rollback/abort semantics, stable gate evaluation under telemetry lag, and audit export readiness. Decision: GO for limited beta with guardrails and explicit hold/force-promote controls restricted to admin roles; no broad enablement until SIEM export and small-pool safeguards are verified.

title: Novum Health - Self-Serve API Quickstart & Hosted/Dedicated Q&A
preview:
[00:00] Ethan Park: Hey everyone, thanks for blocking time. Quick intro — I'm Ethan, AE on the Redwood team. Maya is our solutions engineer, she'll do a quick walkthrough.
[00:12] Rajiv Menon: Thanks Ethan. We wanted to understand how fast we can get up and running with Redwood's hosted API and what the tradeoffs are if we move to Dedicated or Private later.
[00:22] Maya Singh: Great, thanks Rajiv. Today we'll cover the self-serve quickstart, free credit path — time to first token — and then typical migration paths to Dedicated or Private if you need VPC or on-prem.
[00:33] Laura Gomez: High level context: we're building clinical triage assist, low latency for live chat is ideal, throughput maybe 50 qps during peak, latency SLO around 150-200ms per response. Data residency and PHI guard rails are questions.
[00:48] Samir Patel: And from infra, we need to know network egress, certs, SSO, and how RBAC/audit logs work. Also curious about predictable cost — we hate surprises.
[00:59] Maya Singh: Totally. Quick qualification: are you planning to start with hosted API for dev/test or do you require isolation from day one?
[01:07] Rajiv Menon: Prefer hosted first — want to try the quickstart and use the free credits — but we may need Dedicated for production if cost or data residency become blockers.
[01:16] Ethan Park: That's a standard path. The self-serve flow: sign up, redeem trial credits, run the curl or SDK quickstart — you can get your first token in under 5 minutes if you have a key and sample prompt. We have templates for health prompts that others used.
[01:30] Laura Gomez: Under five minutes? Curious what 'under 5' assumes — do we need to install anything or open firewall ports?
[01:37] Maya Singh: No installs for hosted. It's REST or our SDKs (Python/JS). For private you'll need network setup, but hosted is just outbound HTTPS. If you want WebSocket streaming, that's supported too — that's where latency shape matters.
[01:49] Samir Patel: What about SSO? We use Okta and need SCIM provisioning and SAML.
[01:55] Ethan Park: We integrate with SAML/SSO and can do SCIM for user provisioning. On enterprise seats we support enforced SSO and org-level RBAC.
[02:03] Rajiv Menon: Security — SOC 2? ISO? HSM?
[02:07] Maya Singh: We are SOC 2 Type II. For key management in Private deployments you can bring your own KMS/HSM. Hosted has envelope encryption and customer-controlled keys for certain tiers, we can share the security pack after.
[02:18] Laura Gomez: Good. Can you walk us through the quickstart steps live?
[02:21] Maya Singh: Sure, I'll narrate: Step one, sign up on console, confirm email. Step two, create API key — we show it once in the UI, copy to env. Step three, run curl -X POST to the /v1/generate endpoint with prompt. SDKs have helper 'first_token' calls.
[02:35] Maya Singh: You can also import a prompt set into the console for evaluation and see token estimates, cost projections. We surface model latency profiles so you can pick a cheaper quantized model for lower cost or a higher-accuracy model for sensitive triage.
[02:49] Rajiv Menon: That model catalog — do you host open weights or Redwood models? How are we billed across model choices?
[02:55] Ethan Park: We host curated open models and Redwood-verified profiles. Billing is per-token for hosted, with different rates per model tier. Dedicated moves to committed capacity pricing — essentially reserved GPUs with a monthly commitment and predictable throughput.
[03:10] Samir Patel: On Dedicated, do we still call the same API? How big is the integration delta?
[03:15] Maya Singh: Same API surface. For Dedicated you'll get a dedicated endpoint or DNS and we give you configuration options — autoscaling, burst policy. From an engineering perspective it's a config flip plus some VPC networking if you want private connectivity.
[03:27] Laura Gomez: Can you summarize time to migrate from hosted POC to Dedicated prod?
[03:31] Ethan Park: Typical timeline is 4–8 weeks depending on compliance and traffic shaping. If you need Private (VPC/on-prem), add planning for network and KMS, usually 6–12 weeks. These are ballpark.
[03:44] Rajiv Menon: Cost concern: with 50 qps at peak, what do we expect per-token cost? We want to compare OpenAI and Anthropic.
[03:52] Ethan Park: We shared a cost spreadsheet earlier with comparable numbers. Generally, with batching and KV caching you can reduce per-token cost significantly. We'll run a quick estimate in the follow-up based on your sample prompt set.
[04:05] Samir Patel: About batching — if we need low tail-latency, how aggressive is batching? Can we set latencies as a policy?
[04:12] Maya Singh: Yes — adjustable batching windows and dynamic batching. You can set SLO targets and the runtime optimizes batching vs cost. For ultra-low tail-latency you can reduce batch windows or use a higher-priority lane (at cost).
[04:25] Laura Gomez: For PHI, we likely need Private or at least Dedicated with strict data controls. Is data retention configurable on hosted?
[04:33] Ethan Park: Hosted has retention and opt-out controls for enterprise. For regulated PHI workflows, many customers choose Dedicated or Private so they control logs and retention. We can provide a retention policy and audit logs in hosted enterprise tiers.
[04:45] Rajiv Menon: How about end-to-end latency: what do you see for 2-4k token responses? Our triage answers are short, typically 150-300 tokens.
[04:54] Maya Singh: For 150–300 tokens you'll typically see 80–200ms per token depending on model and instance type; end-to-end usually in the 120–400ms range for optimized models. We'll benchmark with your prompts to be precise.
[05:06] Laura Gomez: Any pitfalls during quickstart you've seen?
[05:09] Ethan Park: Common ones: not storing API key securely, not accounting for tokenization differences, and forgetting rate limits — we have per-key quotas but they are tunable. Also people confuse 'first token' streaming vs full response.
[05:22] Rajiv Menon: Can we request dedicated capacity for an upcoming pilot window? We have an internal deadline end of Q2.
[05:28] Ethan Park: Yes, we can provision pilot Dedicated capacity but that often implies a short contract. Alternatively, we can vertically scale hosted for a pilot to avoid commitment. We'll include both options in the follow-up.
[05:39] Samir Patel: Network question — for a Private deployment behind our VPC, how does health checks and observability work?
[05:45] Maya Singh: We ship observability hooks and a console with per-route latency/cost tracing. For Private, we can also stream logs to your SIEM and we provide tracing integrations. Health checks run internally and we expose a local health endpoint for your infra.
[05:58] Laura Gomez: About SAML and SCIM — how long between contract and full SSO setup?
[06:02] Ethan Park: Typically a few days to configure SSO; for SCIM provisioning it depends on the IdP but usually under two weeks end-to-end.

title: Fjord Labs — Reserved Pool Surge Play & Compliance Intake
preview:
Meeting header:
Date/Time: 2025-03-07 16:00 PT
Duration: 42 minutes
Attendees: Mia Thompson (Redwood AE); Ethan Park (Redwood SE); Anika Soren (CTO, Fjord Labs); Marcus Lee (Head of MLOps); Priya Nair (Security); Diego Ramos (SRE)

Summary: Intro + high level requirements for reserved GPU pool, isolation and burst behavior. Heavy focus on compliance (KMS, audit logs), predictable tail latency, and a 2-day benchmark to validate warm pool sizing.

Topics: reserved pools, tenant pinning, hotpool warm strategies, pre-warm vs on-demand, eviction behavior, fallback to hosted model variants, SSO/KMS/H S M integrations, SOC2/ISO questions, pricing tiers and commitment length.

Action items (captured): see metadata list.

Transcript:
[00:00] Mia: Alright, thanks everyone for joining, really appreciate it. Quick intro — I'm Mia from Redwood, AE, and this is Ethan, our SE. We'll run through your reserved capacity needs and compliance asks.
[00:14] Anika: Hi, thanks. I'll jump in — Fjord Labs is handling a lot more real-time doc proceses for maritime logistics, we're seeing spikes during manifest processing windows and need reserved capacity for predictable SLAs.
[00:30] Marcus: Yeah, our 99th percentile latency right now is a bit all over, especially when batch jobs overlap with live inference. We're hoping dedicated GPUs will stabilize that.
[00:45] Ethan: Got it. When you say spikes, can you quantify — rps, tokens per request, typical sequence lengths? Even ballparks help.
[00:52] Marcus: Typical live requests are 200-600 tokens. Peak windows see up to 120 rps across a handful of tenants, but it's bursty — short 2-10 minute storms when port manifests hit.
[01:10] Diego: And we also do nightly batch runs that run heavy embeddings — that's a separate pool ideally.
[01:20] Mia: Cool — so there are two workload classes: real-time low-latency and batch embedding heavy throughput. Makes sense for pool separation.
[01:30] Priya: From security side — we need KMS integration, customer-managed keys preferred, and full audit trails. SOC2 is table stakes and we will need an architecture review.
[01:43] Ethan: Redwood supports customer KMS for Private deployments and we can integrate with your cloud KMS or our HSM workflows for Dedicated, can document the KMS handoff.
[01:52] Priya: When you say HSM, is that your H S M or ours? We have an HSM requirement for keys used in PII handling.
[01:59] Ethan: Good callout. We can support Bring-Your-Own-KMS and tie into an H S M-backed key in your account for Private. For Hosted/dedicated we also have a KMS bridge — we'll send the KMS integration doc.
[02:18] Anika: Important — we need tenant isolation guarantees. Multi-tenant is ok if we can pin certain customers to isolated nodes for higher-risk customers.
[02:29] Ethan: Yep, dedicated node pinning is supported — we can reserve GPU nodes and pin tenants or allocate a hotpool for critical customers. Hotpool pre-warm reduces cold-start tokens.
[02:40] Marcus: How do you handle cache warmness? We use KV cache heavily for sessioned agents.
[02:47] Ethan: We have prefix caching and KV cache reuse possibilities; small detail — sometimes transcripts show 'kv cash' in our docs, so apologies for the spelling in earlier docs, but it's the same cache. The hotpool can warm the KV cache by keeping KV slots alive between requests for pinned tenants.
[03:06] Diego: Eviction behavior? If the hotpool is full and another tenant needs capacity during a burst, what's the policy?
[03:13] Ethan: We typically define eviction tiers. Reserved tenants first; then best-effort warm pool; then on-demand fallback to hosted model variants. For strict isolation you can request node fencing so no eviction for pinned nodes.
[03:28] Priya: Node fencing — does that mean those GPUs are physically or virtually isolated? We need assurance on data separation and audit trails.
[03:36] Ethan: For Dedicated we can allocate isolated GPU pools (tenant-fenced VMs) so you get OS-level separation. We'll include audit log sample entries in the security pack.
[03:48] Anika: What about prewarm sizing? We're not sure how many GPUs to reserve.
[03:54] Mia: For sizing, our usual approach is a 2-day benchmark with your traffic replay to show the warm pool hit rates and 99th percentile latency. We'll run scenarios that vary number of reserved nodes and warm pool sizes and recommend a commit.
[04:11] Marcus: We'd want that benchmark to include our actual prompt templates and also to simulate the manifest storms.
[04:17] Ethan: Absolutely — we'll need a sample workload artifact, ideally a 24-hour condensed replay plus a manifest storm file. We'll provide the harness and you can run it in your environment or ours.
[04:30] Diego: If we run it in your environment, are we sending sensitive data? We prefer to sanitize locally and send synthetic traces.
[04:36] Priya: We'll sanitize PII and use obfuscated manifests for the benchmark.
[04:41] Mia: Works. We'll make a benchmark plan and include guidance on synthetic vs redacted data.
[04:48] Anika: Pricing — we're evaluating commit lengths. We like the idea of a smaller base reserve plus a burst pool. What's the tradeoff?
[04:56] Mia: Tradeoff is cost predictability vs headroom. Base reserve gives you guaranteed throughput and lower unit cost; warm/burst pools are cheaper to hold but you pay more per token when bursting. We can model scenarios — I'll share tiered pricing.
[05:10] Marcus: How does fallback to hosted models work if we exhaust capacity? Do you have automatic routing?
[05:16] Ethan: We have smart routing policies — you can set failover to compatible model variants in the hosted fleet. That preserves availability but may change latency and cost. We can configure automatic fallback thresholds and a billing flag so you can see when fallbacks occur.
[05:33] Marcus: Good, we need visibility for billing reconciliations.
[05:36] Mia: Console provides per-route/model dashboards with token-level cost, latency and a fallback trace.
[05:43] Diego: Any GKE / K8s specifics if we go on-prem later?
[05:47] Ethan: For Private on-prem we have a Kubernetes operator and an appliance image. For Dedicated in cloud we manage the infra but for on-prem you'd run their nodes with the operator. We'll share the ops checklist.
[05:59] Priya: SOC2 + ISO — what evidence do you provide and will we get an architecture review?
[06:05] Mia: We'll share our SOC2 Type II report, ISO docs and can schedule an architecture review with our security team. We'll also include sample audit logs showing access, KMS events, and model version rollouts.
[06:17] Anika: Model rollouts — we need canary and automatic fallback on regressions.
[06:22] Ethan: Rollouts are supported — canary, A/B, and automatic fallback policies if quality metrics drop. You can pin a model version per route.
[06:31] Marcus: Regarding quantization and perf — we run Llama-like models and are open to quantization if impact on quality is low. How do you recommend?
[06:40] Ethan: We provide quantization profiles with expected latency/cost deltas and can run quality regression tests against your prompt set during the benchmark. Often q4_0 gives big cost wins with minor quality impact, but depends on your prompts.
[06:55] Diego: Token accounting — do you charge per generated token or per input token too? We need to map to our billing.
[07:00] Mia: Per-token accounting includes both input and output tokens. Console exposure shows per-request and aggregated cost so you can reconcile.
[07:08] Priya: Any special compliance for embeddings? We store embedding vectors temporarily for caching — how long are they retained?
[07:15] Ethan: Default retention for cache is short-term (minutes) but you can configure TTLs. For Dedicated/Private you control retention and can disable persistent storage. We'll document defaults and controls.
[07:29] Anika: Can you do per-tenant RBAC and route-level ACLs?
[07:33] Ethan: Yes, RBAC and token scoping are supported — route-level policies, SAML SSO integration, and token rotation.
[07:41] Marcus: For the warm pool, what's the billing model? Do we pay for reserved nodes even when idle?
[07:47] Mia: Reserved nodes are part of the commit — you pay the reserved capacity fee regardless of utilization. Warm pools can be configured as part of the reserved contract (lower effective cost) or as burst capacity billed when used. We'll show both models.
[07:59] Diego: Can we set hard caps per tenant to avoid noisy neighbor billing surprises?
[08:04] Ethan: Yes, we can enforce per-tenant quotas and soft caps with alerts. Also cost attribution per token helps trace finger-pointing.
[08:14] Anika: SLA — what uptime and latency bets do you make on Dedicated?
[08:19] Mia: We have SLA options for Dedicated with uptime targets and latency SLOs at route-level. Exact numbers depend on commit size and topology; we'll include SLA options in the pricing deck.
[08:30] Priya: Auditing — can we get real-time streaming of audit logs to our SIEM?
[08:36] Ethan: Yes, we can stream logs to your SIEM/export via secure forwarding (your Kafka/Cloud PubSub or S3). We'll include examples.
[08:45] Marcus: Ok, for the benchmark — can you simulate tenant mix where some tenants are pinned and some are burstable? That's our realistic case.
[08:53] Ethan: Absolutely — we'll parameterize the benchmark to include pinned tenants, warm-pool tenants, and cold on-demand tenants. Results will show tail latency per class.
[09:02] Diego: Any limits on model versions per pool? We want to run two models in parallel during canaries.
[09:08] Ethan: No hard limits — you can run multiple model versions. We'll advise on memory footprint and how many concurrent versions fit per GPU type.
[09:17] Anika: Compliance question — will Redwood sign our data processing addendum and subprocessors list?
[09:23] Mia: Yes, we have a DPA template and subprocessors list. We'll send the legal packet.
[09:30] Priya: Encryption at rest — for embeddings and caches, are they encrypted with our key?
[09:35] Ethan: With customer-managed KMS you can encrypt at rest with your key for Private and Dedicated options. We'll document the KMS flow.
[09:44] Marcus: Network egress — if we use Dedicated in your cloud region, does traffic to our backend incur egress? We want to minimize cross-zone costs.
[09:52] Mia: We can recommend region placement to avoid egress and in Private deployments everything stays inside your VPC so egress is minimal. We'll include region placement recommendations.
[10:03] Anika: What's the timeline from contract to reserved nodes live?
[10:08] Mia: Typical delivery is 4-8 weeks depending on commit, isolation needs, and any Private/on-prem requirements. If you need faster we can do an accelerated path with additional onboarding fees.
[10:20] Marcus: Two follow-ups: 1) We'll want a proof-of-concept run to validate 99th percentile during storms; 2) Security will want architecture review.
[10:29] Mia: Sounds good — we'll schedule the PoC benchmark and the security review.
[10:33] Ethan: For the PoC we'll propose a 48-hour window with three scenarios: normal, storm (2–10 minute high rps), and batch overlay. That should surface tail behavior.
[10:42] Diego: Fine with that, and we'll provide sanitized manifests.
[10:47] Anika: Are there common pitfalls you see customers make when sizing warm pools?
[10:51] Ethan: Yes — two big ones: undersizing warm pools leading to frequent fallbacks, and relying only on autoscaling during storms without prewarm which increases tail latency. Also tagging heavy batch work in the same pool as low-latency routes causes jitter; we recommend separate pools.
[11:10] Marcus: For autoscaling policies — are they per-route or per-pool?
[11:14] Ethan: Per-pool with route-level hooks. You can set autoscale based on tokens/sec, queue depth, or 99th percentile latency.
[11:25] Priya: Does Redwood provide runbooks for incident response around noisy tenants or preemption?
[11:31] Mia: Yes, we have runbooks and we can share a sample incident playbook for eviction, fallback, and rollback.
[11:40] Anika: Okay let's recap next steps.
[11:43] Mia: Recap — we'll send: pricing and SLA options, the security pack (SOC2, ISO, DPA, subprocessors), benchmark plan and harness, and a proposed 48-hour PoC schedule. Ethan will scoping warm pool sizes after the benchmark.
[11:58] Priya: I'll send our compliance checklist and confirm H S M requirements.
[12:03] Marcus: We'll prepare the traffic traces and synthetic manifests.
[12:08] Mia: Great — Mia will follow up with calendar invites and docs. Anything else?
[12:13] Diego: Just one small ask — can you include a matrix of performance per GPU type (A100 vs H100 vs next-gen)?
[12:19] Ethan: Yes — we'll include expected p99 token latency and throughput per GPU profile, and quantify quantization tradeoffs.
[12:28] Anika: Thanks everyone. Look forward to the benchmark.
[12:31] Mia: Thanks all. We'll send the materials and get the PoC on the calendar.

End of transcript.

Summary: Intro + high level requirements for reserved GPU pool, isolation and burst behavior. Heavy focus on compliance (KMS, audit logs), predictable tail latency, and a 2-day benchmark to validate warm pool sizing.

Topics: reserved pools, tenant pinning, hotpool warm strategies, pre-warm vs on-demand, eviction behavior, fallback to hosted model variants, SSO/KMS/H S M integrations, SOC2/ISO questions, pricing tiers and commitment length.

Action items (captured): see metadata list.

Transcript:
[00:00] Mia: Alright, thanks everyone for joining, really appreciate it. Quick intro — I'm Mia from Redwood, AE, and this is Ethan, our SE. We'll run through your reserved capacity needs and compliance asks.
[00:14] Anika: Hi, thanks. I'll jump in — Fjord Labs is handling a lot more real-time doc proceses for maritime logistics, we're seeing spikes during manifest processing windows and need reserved capacity for predictable SLAs.
[00:30] Marcus: Yeah, our 99th percentile latency right now is a bit all over, especially when batch jobs overlap with live inference. We're hoping dedicated GPUs will stabilize that.
[00:45] Ethan: Got it. When you say spikes, can you quantify — rps, tokens per request, typical sequence lengths? Even ballparks help.
[00:52] Marcus: Typical live requests are 200-600 tokens. Peak windows see up to 120 rps across a handful of tenants, but it's bursty — short 2-10 minute storms when port manifests hit.
[01:10] Diego: And we also do nightly batch runs that run heavy embeddings — that's a separate pool ideally.
[01:20] Mia: Cool — so there are two workload classes: real-time low-latency and batch embedding heavy throughput. Makes sense for pool separation.
[01:30] Priya: From security side — we need KMS integration, customer-managed keys preferred, and full audit trails. SOC2 is table stakes and we will need an architecture review.
[01:43] Ethan: Redwood supports customer KMS for Private deployments and we can integrate with your cloud KMS or our HSM workflows for Dedicated, can document the KMS handoff.
[01:52] Priya: When you say HSM, is that your H S M or ours? We have an HSM requirement for keys used in PII handling.
[01:59] Ethan: Good callout. We can support Bring-Your-Own-KMS and tie into an H S M-backed key in your account for Private. For Hosted/dedicated we also have a KMS bridge — we'll send the KMS integration doc.
[02:18] Anika: Important — we need tenant isolation guarantees. Multi-tenant is ok if we can pin certain customers to isolated nodes for higher-risk customers.
[02:29] Ethan: Yep, dedicated node pinning is supported — we can reserve GPU nodes and pin tenants or allocate a hotpool for critical customers. Hotpool pre-warm reduces cold-start tokens.
[02:40] Marcus: How do you handle cache warmness? We use KV cache heavily for sessioned agents.
[02:47] Ethan: We have prefix caching and KV cache reuse possibilities; small detail — sometimes transcripts show 'kv cash' in our docs, so apologies for the spelling in earlier docs, but it's the same cache. The hotpool can warm the KV cache by keeping KV slots alive between requests for pinned tenants.
[03:06] Diego: Eviction behavior? If the hotpool is full and another tenant needs capacity during a burst, what's the policy?
[03:13] Ethan: We typically define eviction tiers. Reserved tenants first; then best-effort warm pool; then on-demand fallback to hosted model variants. For strict isolation you can request node fencing so no eviction for pinned nodes.
[03:28] Priya: Node fencing — does that mean those GPUs are physically or virtually isolated? We need assurance on data separation and audit trails.
[03:36] Ethan: For Dedicated we can allocate isolated GPU pools (tenant-fenced VMs) so you get OS-level separation. We'll include audit log sample entries in the security pack.
[03:48] Anika: What about prewarm sizing? We're not sure how many GPUs to reserve.
[03:54] Mia: For sizing, our usual approach is a 2-day benchmark with your traffic replay to show the warm pool hit rates and 99th percentile latency. We'll run scenarios that vary number of reserved nodes and warm pool sizes and recommend a commit.
[04:11] Marcus: We'd want that benchmark to include our actual prompt templates and also to simulate the manifest storms.
[04:17] Ethan: Absolutely — we'll need a sample workload artifact, ideally a 24-hour condensed replay plus a manifest storm file. We'll provide the harness and you can run it in your environment or ours.
[04:30] Diego: If we run it in your environment, are we sending sensitive data? We prefer to sanitize locally and send synthetic traces.
[04:36] Priya: We'll sanitize PII and use obfuscated manifests for the benchmark.
[04:41] Mia: Works. We'll make a benchmark plan and include guidance on synthetic vs redacted data.
[04:48] Anika: Pricing — we're evaluating commit lengths. We like the idea of a smaller base reserve plus a burst pool. What's the tradeoff?
[04:56] Mia: Tradeoff is cost predictability vs headroom. Base reserve gives you guaranteed throughput and lower unit cost; warm/burst pools are cheaper to hold but you pay more per token when bursting. We can model scenarios — I'll share tiered pricing.
[05:10] Marcus: How does fallback to hosted models work if we exhaust capacity? Do you have automatic routing?
[05:16] Ethan: We have smart routing policies — you can set failover to compatible model variants in the hosted fleet. That preserves availability but may change latency and cost. We can configure automatic fallback thresholds and a billing flag so you can see when fallbacks occur.
[05:33] Marcus: Good, we need visibility for billing reconciliations.
[05:36] Mia: Console provides per-route/model dashboards with token-level cost, latency and a fallback trace.
[05:43] Diego: Any GKE / K8s specifics if we go on-prem later?
[05:47] Ethan: For Private on-prem we have a Kubernetes operator and an appliance image. For Dedicated in cloud we manage the infra but for on-prem you'd run their nodes with the operator. We'll share the ops checklist.
[05:59] Priya: SOC2 + ISO — what evidence do you provide and will we get an architecture review?
[06:05] Mia: We'll share our SOC2 Type II report, ISO docs and can schedule an architecture review with our security team. We'll also include sample audit logs showing access, KMS events, and model version rollouts.
[06:17] Anika: Model rollouts — we need canary and automatic fallback on regressions.
[06:22] Ethan: Rollouts are supported — canary, A/B, and automatic fallback policies if quality metrics drop. You can pin a model version per route.
[06:31] Marcus: Regarding quantization and perf — we run Llama-like models and are open to quantization if impact on quality is low. How do you recommend?
[06:40] Ethan: We provide quantization profiles with expected latency/cost deltas and can run quality regression tests against your prompt set during the benchmark. Often q4_0 gives big cost wins with minor quality impact, but depends on your prompts.
[06:55] Diego: Token accounting — do you charge per generated token or per input token too? We need to map to our billing.
[07:00] Mia: Per-token accounting includes both input and output tokens. Console exposure shows per-request and aggregated cost so you can reconcile.
[07:08] Priya: Any special compliance for embeddings? We store embedding vectors temporarily for caching — how long are they retained?
[07:15] Ethan: Default retention for cache is short-term (minutes) but you can configure TTLs. For Dedicated/Private you control retention and can disable persistent storage. We'll document defaults and controls.
[07:29] Anika: Can you do per-tenant RBAC and route-level ACLs?
[07:33] Ethan: Yes, RBAC and token scoping are supported — route-level policies, SAML SSO integration, and token rotation.
[07:41] Marcus: For the warm pool, what's the billing model? Do we pay for reserved nodes even when idle?
[07:47] Mia: Reserved nodes are part of the commit — you pay the reserved capacity fee regardless of utilization. Warm pools can be configured as part of the reserved contract (lower effective cost) or as burst capacity billed when used. We'll show both models.
[07:59] Diego: Can we set hard caps per tenant to avoid noisy neighbor billing surprises?
[08:04] Ethan: Yes, we can enforce per-tenant quotas and soft caps with alerts. Also cost attribution per token helps trace finger-pointing.
[08:14] Anika: SLA — what uptime and latency bets do you make on Dedicated?
[08:19] Mia: We have SLA options for Dedicated with uptime targets and latency SLOs at route-level. Exact numbers depend on commit size and topology; we'll include SLA options in the pricing deck.
[08:30] Priya: Auditing — can we get real-time streaming of audit logs to our SIEM?
[08:36] Ethan: Yes, we can stream logs to your SIEM/export via secure forwarding (your Kafka/Cloud PubSub or S3). We'll include examples.
[08:45] Marcus: Ok, for the benchmark — can you simulate tenant mix where some tenants are pinned and some are burstable? That's our realistic case.
[08:53] Ethan: Absolutely — we'll parameterize the benchmark to include pinned tenants, warm-pool tenants, and cold on-demand tenants. Results will show tail latency per class.
[09:02] Diego: Any limits on model versions per pool? We want to run two models in parallel during canaries.
[09:08] Ethan: No hard limits — you can run multiple model versions. We'll advise on memory footprint and how many concurrent versions fit per GPU type.
[09:17] Anika: Compliance question — will Redwood sign our data processing addendum and subprocessors list?
[09:23] Mia: Yes, we have a DPA template and subprocessors list. We'll send the legal packet.
[09:30] Priya: Encryption at rest — for embeddings and caches, are they encrypted with our key?
[09:35] Ethan: With customer-managed KMS you can encrypt at rest with your key for Private and Dedicated options. We'll document the KMS flow.
[09:44] Marcus: Network egress — if we use Dedicated in your cloud region, does traffic to our backend incur egress? We want to minimize cross-zone costs.
[09:52] Mia: We can recommend region placement to avoid egress and in Private deployments everything stays inside your VPC so egress is minimal. We'll include region placement recommendations.
[10:03] Anika: What's the timeline from contract to reserved nodes live?
[10:08] Mia: Typical delivery is 4-8 weeks depending on commit, isolation needs, and any Private/on-prem requirements. If you need faster we can do an accelerated path with additional onboarding fees.
[10:20] Marcus: Two follow-ups: 1) We'll want a proof-of-concept run to validate 99th percentile during storms; 2) Security will want architecture review.
[10:29] Mia: Sounds good — we'll schedule the PoC benchmark and the security review.
[10:33] Ethan: For the PoC we'll propose a 48-hour window with three scenarios: normal, storm (2–10 minute high rps), and batch overlay. That should surface tail behavior.
[10:42] Diego: Fine with that, and we'll provide sanitized manifests.
[10:47] Anika: Are there common pitfalls you see customers make when sizing warm pools?
[10:51] Ethan: Yes — two big ones: undersizing warm pools leading to frequent fallbacks, and relying only on autoscaling during storms without prewarm which increases tail latency. Also tagging heavy batch work in the same pool as low-latency routes causes jitter; we recommend separate pools.
[11:10] Marcus: For autoscaling policies — are they per-route or per-pool?
[11:14] Ethan: Per-pool with route-level hooks. You can set autoscale based on tokens/sec, queue depth, or 99th percentile latency.
[11:25] Priya: Does Redwood provide runbooks for incident response around noisy tenants or preemption?
[11:31] Mia: Yes, we have runbooks and we can share a sample incident playbook for eviction, fallback, and rollback.
[11:40] Anika: Okay let's recap next steps.
[11:43] Mia: Recap — we'll send: pricing and SLA options, the security pack (SOC2, ISO, DPA, subprocessors), benchmark plan and harness, and a proposed 48-hour PoC schedule. Ethan will scoping warm pool sizes after the benchmark.
[11:58] Priya: I'll send our compliance checklist and confirm H S M requirements.
[12:03] Marcus: We'll prepare the traffic traces and synthetic manifests.
[12:08] Mia: Great — Mia will follow up with calendar invites and docs. Anything else?
[12:13] Diego: Just one small ask — can you include a matrix of performance per GPU type (A100 vs H100 vs next-gen)?
[12:19] Ethan: Yes — we'll include expected p99 token latency and throughput per GPU profile, and quantify quantization tradeoffs.
[12:28] Anika: Thanks everyone. Look forward to the benchmark.
[12:31] Mia: Thanks all. We'll send the materials and get the PoC on the calendar.

End of transcript.

title: Northwind CX 1 Tool Calling Reliability Deep Dive
preview:
Meeting: Northwind CX 1 Tool Calling Reliability Deep Dive
Date: 2025-03-05
Time: 2:00 PM ET
Duration: 46 min
Attendees: Avery Johnson (Redwood), Ethan Park (Redwood), Chloe Martin (Redwood), Sam Ortega (Northwind), Priya Desai (Northwind), Dana Wu (Northwind)

Summary (auto):
Northwind CX reports intermittent tool call failures due to invalid JSON and occasional plain-text arguments. Redwood proposes prompt placement changes and enabling strict JSON validation mode on the route.

Topics (auto):
- Tool calling output format
- JSON parsing failures
- Streaming vs non-streaming
- Retry strategy

[00:00] Avery Johnson: cool, thanks everyone. We saw the ticket from Chloe. Want to dig into the tool calling failures.
[00:22] Sam Ortega: yeah. Its not frequent but when it happens its a hard crash for us. The tool args come back not as JSON. Sometimes its like  just a sentence.
[00:45] Ethan Park: got it. Are you using streaming?
[00:49] Priya Desai: mostly non-streaming. We tried streaming earlier but disabled it. Even without streaming we still see some cases.
[01:12] Chloe Martin: the examples you sent had `tool_calls.arguments` like "Call search_tickets with query=refund failed". Thats what youre seeing?
[01:24] Sam Ortega: exactly. Our parser expects JSON like `{"query":"..."}`.
[02:05] Ethan Park: weve seen this when the tool schema gets pushed out of context. The model still chooses tool call but doesnt follow JSON formatting.
[02:41] Dana Wu: so is Redwood supposed to guarantee its always valid JSON?
[03:02] Avery Johnson: the contract is that tool-call arguments are JSON; in practice we recommend validating and retrying once because models can be stochastic. Were also adding server-side validation in a strict mode.
[04:10] Priya Desai: we cant do a bunch of retries due to rate limits and latency.
[04:22] Ethan Park: we can enable `tool_args_json_mode=strict` on your route. It does a post-generation JSON validation and if invalid it can regenerate with a stronger constraint. Its not perfect but reduces the tail cases.
[05:18] Sam Ortega: ok. Also, do we need to change our prompt?
[05:33] Ethan Park: yes  move the tool schema earlier and add explicit "STRICT JSON" language. And keep the tool args schema short.
[06:40] Chloe Martin: well share a small snippet.

[18:05] Sam Ortega: one more thing. In the past we also got trailing commas, but that was with streaming.
[18:18] Ethan Park: yeah, we fixed a streaming arg coalescing bug recently in us-east. For eu-west, we havent seen it as much but well keep an eye.

[41:10] Avery Johnson: recap: well enable strict mode on your route and share known-issues guidance; youll adjust prompt placement and report updated fail rate.
[41:45] Sam Ortega: sounds good.

title: Postmortem: March 3rd outage review with AcmeCloud — follow-up & mitigations
preview:
Meeting header: 2025-03-05 15:00 UTC | Duration: 46 minutes | Recording: internal/ff/ff-20250305-acme.mp3
Attendees: Ethan Park (Redwood AE), Sofia Martinez (Redwood SE), Rajiv Patel (Redwood CSM); Dana Ruiz (AcmeCloud VP Eng), Liam O'Connor (SRE Lead), Priya Nair (Security), Tom Becker (Procurement)

Summary:
- Purpose: walk through root cause of Mar 3 outage, confirm mitigations, agree next steps and timelines for reliability improvements.
- High-level root cause identified as cascading memory pressure in the batching service + an edge-case in autoscaler thresholds; KV cache thrash amplified fault domain.
- Short-term mitigations: tuned autoscaler, emergency patch to batching memory accounting, temporary traffic shaping / rate-limit policy.
- Long-term actions: circuit breaker pattern, better observability around prefix/K V cache hits, engineer runbooks and chaos tests.

Topics: timeline, root cause, mitigations applied, security implications, SLAs & credits, next steps

Transcript:
[00:00] Ethan (Redwood AE): okay everyone, thanks for joining. I know this is a follow up from the post mort - the outage on March third. we wanted to make sure we captured the final RCA and action items. um, starting off, Dana do you want to open with what you saw on your side?
[00:12] Dana (AcmeCloud): yeah thanks Ethan. so, from our side we started seeing elevated 5xxs and timeouts around 10:42 UTC. it was initially intermittent but by 10:55 the spike became sustained and our half of the traffic hit degraded latencies. we routed to fallback but it wasn't perfect.
[00:30] Liam (SRE): just to add, our infra graphs show memory climb on the inference batching nodes — the node OOMs didn't occur but GC latencies exploded and the qps dropped. we also saw a larger than expected number of cache misses which i think made the problem worse, so the KV cache ended up thrashing.
[00:45] Sofia (Redwood SE): when I say KV cache, I mean the prefix cache on the serving layer. our logs indicate increased mem allocations from the batching subsystem when sequence length variance was high — the batching window grew bigger than usual.
[01:02] Priya (Security): was there any evidence of compromised keys or KMS failures during that window? we saw a small blip in decrypt latency but cannot confirm it's related.
[01:12] Ethan: good question. Sofia, did you see anything in the logs about KMS?
[01:17] Sofia: no direct KMS errors from Redwood. the slight decrypt latency was correlated with the downstream region saturating iops; so we saw a tail latency on KMS calls but no denied decrypts. still, we'll include that in the audit.
[01:29] Tom (Procurement): quick — sorry to jump in — how will this affect contract credits / SLA? we had revenue risk during the few hours.
[01:37] Rajiv (CSM): we can open a formal SLA review. Ethan will own the postmortem and we'll route to finance for credit assessment after we complete RCA.
[01:47] Dana: timeline please, we need the full log set + cause attribution.
[01:52] Ethan: yes, we'll share annotated logs and a timeline by Friday.

[02:05] Sofia: to dig into the root cause — the sequence was: a traffic pattern change yielded many longer sequences in a short timespan, batching window and merge algorithm created oversized batches, memory accounting in the batching queue didn't trigger early shedding, so transient memory pressure grew. because of prefix cache misses — K V cache misses — the runtime had to recompute more keys and allocate more memory, amplifying the issue.
[02:30] Liam: and to clarify, the autoscaler thresholds were tuned for CPU and throughput, not this particular mem spike. the HPA didn't react fast enough because memory metrics are sampled at a lower cadence, so the scale out lagged.
[02:46] Ethan: so there's two failure domains: batching memory accounting and autoscaler cadence/thresholds. that aligns with what we saw in the traces.
[02:55] Dana: correct. and importantly, our app retries were exponential and that increased load as well.
[03:05] Sofia: yep retry storms are classic — we need better client-side guidance and maybe a token bucket server-side limit for burst protection.
[03:15] Liam: question to Redwood — after you deployed the emergency patch at 11:30, we saw latencies fall, but there was a residual elevated error until 13:00. what was done during that window?
[03:26] Ethan: right. immediate steps were: roll out a memory accounting fix to batching that forces early batch trimming under memory pressure; temporarily lower max batch size; enable a soft rate-limit per-tenant. we also nudged autoscaler to a more aggressive profile which helped. full stabilization took until ~13:00 as backlogged requests cleared.
[03:45] Priya: were any user payloads or customer data logged to plaintext while the sys was degraded? compliance is worried about spillover.
[03:51] Sofia: we did a quick sweep and didn't find PII or raw customer prompts logged beyond normal debug lines. still, we'll run a focused grep across the buffer windows and share results.
[04:05] Dana: thanks. we need that for our audit.

[04:15] Ethan: i also want to call out a weird mis-parse in the monitoring — some alerts were suppressed by a dedupe rule tuned for flapping which in hindsight hid the early warning.
[04:26] Rajiv: can you expand on suppressed alerts? that seems big.
[04:29] Ethan: sure. the dedupe had a 30m silence window for the metric stream we relied on. during the incipient period alerts got collapsed; engineers didn't see the small bump. we'll remove that suppression and add a separate alert with lower threshold and different dedupe rules.
[04:44] Liam: that's alarming — we should get those alert changes ASAP.

[05:00] Sofia: for mitigations, we pushed: 1) batching memory accounting patch; 2) reduced max_batch_size from 64 to 32 temporarily; 3) temporary tenant rate-limits and token-bucket enforcement; 4) tuning autoscaler to sample memory every 15s instead of 60s and scale faster.
[05:20] Dana: which of these are permanent?
[05:23] Sofia: memory accounting patch is permanent. reduced max_batch_size is temporary until we validate the accounting fix under load. token-bucket/rate-limit will be productized as a configurable protection. autoscaler sampling change requires more testing in prod-like env but we recommended moving to the 15s cadence.
[05:40] Tom: from procurement point, will there be a change in cost? if you increase autoscale aggressiveness, monthly bills could spike.
[05:48] Rajiv: we'll quantify cost impact before committing; the priority is reliability and then we balance cost with scheduled burst policies.

[06:10] Ethan: let's talk about long-term reliability work: circuit breaker pattern on the client side, better KV cache eviction policies (LRU was beating us), a backpressure signal in the API to indicate degraded mode, and more realistic chaos-tests.
[06:28] Liam: chaos tests — can we run a workload replay against a staging cluster that mirrors the long sequence payloads? we want to validate accounting changes under the same sequence distribution.
[06:37] Sofia: yes, we can capture a sanitized sample trace and run it in the Redwood staging harness. we'll need the config snapshot from Acme to reproduce exact settings.
[06:46] Dana: we'll provide sanitized traces, but we need an NDA addendum around those traces, is that possible?
[06:51] Priya: also encryption: can we confirm that logs with traces are encrypted at rest and access-controlled?
[06:58] Ethan: yes logs are encrypted; we'll provide access ACLs and the S3 bucket policy. Rajiv will coordinate access.

[07:10] Sofia: another item: we noticed a model variant mismatch on a minority of requests — there was a routing fallback to an older quantized variant which had slightly different memory footprint and that increased pressure for a short period. there's a bug in our model routing compatibility check.
[07:29] Liam: that would explain why some nodes spiked worse than others.
[07:33] Ethan: good catch. we'll include model-routing compatibility check in the remediation.

[07:40] Dana: what about monitoring — what dashboards should we watch? we want a sprint-ready list.
[07:45] Sofia: top items: batching queue memory, per-node resident set size (RSS), prefix cache hit rate, batch size distribution, KMS decrypt latency, per-tenant request error rates, and autoscaler decision logs. we'll share a dashboard pack.

[08:02] Priya: from security POV, we also want proof that during the incident there were no anomalous API keys used. can you run an auth audit and show tokens used?
[08:11] Ethan: affirmative. we'll provide auth logs filtered for the incident window.

[08:20] Tom: if we accept the mitigation plan, what's the timeline to be back to pre-incident risk levels?
[08:26] Sofia: short term (week): deploy permanent memory accounting patch across clusters, adjust autoscaler cadence, create runbook. medium term (4-8 weeks): circuit breaker + token-bucket + dashboard pack and chaos test. long term (3 months): expanded canary testing, refine model routing checks, automated failover.

[08:45] Dana: okay that sounds reasonable. please also document the customer-facing change log so our product teams can align.
[08:52] Rajiv: we'll publish a changelog and a timeline for expected rollouts.

[09:00] Ethan: i want to call out an operational note — we found a race inside the batching code where the accounting counter could briefly undercount during concurrent merges; fix is in the patch. it's subtle and not immediately obvious in the raw logs.
[09:15] Sofia: that's why the staged chaos tests are critical. also, we'll add better unit tests around concurrent batch merges.

[09:28] Liam: are there any customer-facing mitigations we can toggle if we see early signs? like an explicit degraded-mode header clients can respect?
[09:36] Ethan: yes we can emit an X-Model-Degraded header with a code and recommended backoff. we'll design that so it doesn't break integrations.

[09:48] Dana: from a legal/compliance angle, we will likely ask for a formal signed postmortem. can Redwood provide a doc with timelines and root-cause analysis?
[09:56] Ethan: yes, we'll deliver a signed postmortem and logs bundle.

[10:05] (transcript skips ahead as team discusses log packaging)
[10:20] Sofia: packaging logs — we'll include trace IDs, span times, sampling rates, and the raw batch sizes by request id when available. note: some request ids were stripped for PII.
[10:33] Priya: thank you.

[10:40] Ethan: let's surface any open questions or risks.
[10:43] Dana: risk: repeated sequences during peak could still expose us until autoscaler is fully tested. also, we need clear SLA credit terms.
[10:53] Tom: procurement needs the SLA clause and a timeline for any credits or PRs.
[11:00] Rajiv: we'll start the SLA review parallel to the RCA.

[11:07] Sofia: open technical risk: model-routing compatibility check needs a small release; we expect patch in 2 weeks.
[11:14] Ethan: open operational risk: alert dedupe rule will be removed in 24-48 hours and replaced with finer-grained dedupe.

[11:22] Dana: question — is this incident likely to recur with other customers?
[11:27] Ethan: the root cause is a workload-specific interaction (long sequences + retry storm) but the mitigations will be rolled out globally because it touches the runtime and autoscaler. so other customers would have been at risk.

[11:40] Liam: can Redwood share a quick patch timeline so we can plan change windows?
[11:45] Sofia: yes: memory accounting patch is live on canary now, will go to blue/green next Wednesday (2025-03-12) pending staging replay. autoscaler tweaks to follow after internal stress tests, target 2025-03-19.

[12:05] Ethan: Ok — final recap: we'll share the annotated postmortem and logs by 2025-03-07; Sofia will publish the runbook and draft circuit breaker by 2025-03-12; Priya and Liam will exchange audit and config snapshots by 2025-03-10; Rajiv/Ethan schedule follow-up 2025-03-19.
