text:
Meeting header: 2025-03-05 15:00 UTC | Duration: 46 minutes | Recording: internal/ff/ff-20250305-acme.mp3
Attendees: Ethan Park (Redwood AE), Sofia Martinez (Redwood SE), Rajiv Patel (Redwood CSM); Dana Ruiz (AcmeCloud VP Eng), Liam O'Connor (SRE Lead), Priya Nair (Security), Tom Becker (Procurement)

Summary:
- Purpose: walk through root cause of Mar 3 outage, confirm mitigations, agree next steps and timelines for reliability improvements.
- High-level root cause identified as cascading memory pressure in the batching service + an edge-case in autoscaler thresholds; KV cache thrash amplified fault domain.
- Short-term mitigations: tuned autoscaler, emergency patch to batching memory accounting, temporary traffic shaping / rate-limit policy.
- Long-term actions: circuit breaker pattern, better observability around prefix/K V cache hits, engineer runbooks and chaos tests.

Topics: timeline, root cause, mitigations applied, security implications, SLAs & credits, next steps

Transcript:
[00:00] Ethan (Redwood AE): okay everyone, thanks for joining. I know this is a follow up from the post mort - the outage on March third. we wanted to make sure we captured the final RCA and action items. um, starting off, Dana do you want to open with what you saw on your side?
[00:12] Dana (AcmeCloud): yeah thanks Ethan. so, from our side we started seeing elevated 5xxs and timeouts around 10:42 UTC. it was initially intermittent but by 10:55 the spike became sustained and our half of the traffic hit degraded latencies. we routed to fallback but it wasn't perfect.
[00:30] Liam (SRE): just to add, our infra graphs show memory climb on the inference batching nodes — the node OOMs didn't occur but GC latencies exploded and the qps dropped. we also saw a larger than expected number of cache misses which i think made the problem worse, so the KV cache ended up thrashing.
[00:45] Sofia (Redwood SE): when I say KV cache, I mean the prefix cache on the serving layer. our logs indicate increased mem allocations from the batching subsystem when sequence length variance was high — the batching window grew bigger than usual.
[01:02] Priya (Security): was there any evidence of compromised keys or KMS failures during that window? we saw a small blip in decrypt latency but cannot confirm it's related.
[01:12] Ethan: good question. Sofia, did you see anything in the logs about KMS?
[01:17] Sofia: no direct KMS errors from Redwood. the slight decrypt latency was correlated with the downstream region saturating iops; so we saw a tail latency on KMS calls but no denied decrypts. still, we'll include that in the audit.
[01:29] Tom (Procurement): quick — sorry to jump in — how will this affect contract credits / SLA? we had revenue risk during the few hours.
[01:37] Rajiv (CSM): we can open a formal SLA review. Ethan will own the postmortem and we'll route to finance for credit assessment after we complete RCA.
[01:47] Dana: timeline please, we need the full log set + cause attribution.
[01:52] Ethan: yes, we'll share annotated logs and a timeline by Friday.

[02:05] Sofia: to dig into the root cause — the sequence was: a traffic pattern change yielded many longer sequences in a short timespan, batching window and merge algorithm created oversized batches, memory accounting in the batching queue didn't trigger early shedding, so transient memory pressure grew. because of prefix cache misses — K V cache misses — the runtime had to recompute more keys and allocate more memory, amplifying the issue.
[02:30] Liam: and to clarify, the autoscaler thresholds were tuned for CPU and throughput, not this particular mem spike. the HPA didn't react fast enough because memory metrics are sampled at a lower cadence, so the scale out lagged.
[02:46] Ethan: so there's two failure domains: batching memory accounting and autoscaler cadence/thresholds. that aligns with what we saw in the traces.
[02:55] Dana: correct. and importantly, our app retries were exponential and that increased load as well.
[03:05] Sofia: yep retry storms are classic — we need better client-side guidance and maybe a token bucket server-side limit for burst protection.
[03:15] Liam: question to Redwood — after you deployed the emergency patch at 11:30, we saw latencies fall, but there was a residual elevated error until 13:00. what was done during that window?
[03:26] Ethan: right. immediate steps were: roll out a memory accounting fix to batching that forces early batch trimming under memory pressure; temporarily lower max batch size; enable a soft rate-limit per-tenant. we also nudged autoscaler to a more aggressive profile which helped. full stabilization took until ~13:00 as backlogged requests cleared.
[03:45] Priya: were any user payloads or customer data logged to plaintext while the sys was degraded? compliance is worried about spillover.
[03:51] Sofia: we did a quick sweep and didn't find PII or raw customer prompts logged beyond normal debug lines. still, we'll run a focused grep across the buffer windows and share results.
[04:05] Dana: thanks. we need that for our audit.

[04:15] Ethan: i also want to call out a weird mis-parse in the monitoring — some alerts were suppressed by a dedupe rule tuned for flapping which in hindsight hid the early warning.
[04:26] Rajiv: can you expand on suppressed alerts? that seems big.
[04:29] Ethan: sure. the dedupe had a 30m silence window for the metric stream we relied on. during the incipient period alerts got collapsed; engineers didn't see the small bump. we'll remove that suppression and add a separate alert with lower threshold and different dedupe rules.
[04:44] Liam: that's alarming — we should get those alert changes ASAP.

[05:00] Sofia: for mitigations, we pushed: 1) batching memory accounting patch; 2) reduced max_batch_size from 64 to 32 temporarily; 3) temporary tenant rate-limits and token-bucket enforcement; 4) tuning autoscaler to sample memory every 15s instead of 60s and scale faster.
[05:20] Dana: which of these are permanent?
[05:23] Sofia: memory accounting patch is permanent. reduced max_batch_size is temporary until we validate the accounting fix under load. token-bucket/rate-limit will be productized as a configurable protection. autoscaler sampling change requires more testing in prod-like env but we recommended moving to the 15s cadence.
[05:40] Tom: from procurement point, will there be a change in cost? if you increase autoscale aggressiveness, monthly bills could spike.
[05:48] Rajiv: we'll quantify cost impact before committing; the priority is reliability and then we balance cost with scheduled burst policies.

[06:10] Ethan: let's talk about long-term reliability work: circuit breaker pattern on the client side, better KV cache eviction policies (LRU was beating us), a backpressure signal in the API to indicate degraded mode, and more realistic chaos-tests.
[06:28] Liam: chaos tests — can we run a workload replay against a staging cluster that mirrors the long sequence payloads? we want to validate accounting changes under the same sequence distribution.
[06:37] Sofia: yes, we can capture a sanitized sample trace and run it in the Redwood staging harness. we'll need the config snapshot from Acme to reproduce exact settings.
[06:46] Dana: we'll provide sanitized traces, but we need an NDA addendum around those traces, is that possible?
[06:51] Priya: also encryption: can we confirm that logs with traces are encrypted at rest and access-controlled?
[06:58] Ethan: yes logs are encrypted; we'll provide access ACLs and the S3 bucket policy. Rajiv will coordinate access.

[07:10] Sofia: another item: we noticed a model variant mismatch on a minority of requests — there was a routing fallback to an older quantized variant which had slightly different memory footprint and that increased pressure for a short period. there's a bug in our model routing compatibility check.
[07:29] Liam: that would explain why some nodes spiked worse than others.
[07:33] Ethan: good catch. we'll include model-routing compatibility check in the remediation.

[07:40] Dana: what about monitoring — what dashboards should we watch? we want a sprint-ready list.
[07:45] Sofia: top items: batching queue memory, per-node resident set size (RSS), prefix cache hit rate, batch size distribution, KMS decrypt latency, per-tenant request error rates, and autoscaler decision logs. we'll share a dashboard pack.

[08:02] Priya: from security POV, we also want proof that during the incident there were no anomalous API keys used. can you run an auth audit and show tokens used?
[08:11] Ethan: affirmative. we'll provide auth logs filtered for the incident window.

[08:20] Tom: if we accept the mitigation plan, what's the timeline to be back to pre-incident risk levels?
[08:26] Sofia: short term (week): deploy permanent memory accounting patch across clusters, adjust autoscaler cadence, create runbook. medium term (4-8 weeks): circuit breaker + token-bucket + dashboard pack and chaos test. long term (3 months): expanded canary testing, refine model routing checks, automated failover.

[08:45] Dana: okay that sounds reasonable. please also document the customer-facing change log so our product teams can align.
[08:52] Rajiv: we'll publish a changelog and a timeline for expected rollouts.

[09:00] Ethan: i want to call out an operational note — we found a race inside the batching code where the accounting counter could briefly undercount during concurrent merges; fix is in the patch. it's subtle and not immediately obvious in the raw logs.
[09:15] Sofia: that's why the staged chaos tests are critical. also, we'll add better unit tests around concurrent batch merges.

[09:28] Liam: are there any customer-facing mitigations we can toggle if we see early signs? like an explicit degraded-mode header clients can respect?
[09:36] Ethan: yes we can emit an X-Model-Degraded header with a code and recommended backoff. we'll design that so it doesn't break integrations.

[09:48] Dana: from a legal/compliance angle, we will likely ask for a formal signed postmortem. can Redwood provide a doc with timelines and root-cause analysis?
[09:56] Ethan: yes, we'll deliver a signed postmortem and logs bundle.

[10:05] (transcript skips ahead as team discusses log packaging)
[10:20] Sofia: packaging logs — we'll include trace IDs, span times, sampling rates, and the raw batch sizes by request id when available. note: some request ids were stripped for PII.
[10:33] Priya: thank you.

[10:40] Ethan: let's surface any open questions or risks.
[10:43] Dana: risk: repeated sequences during peak could still expose us until autoscaler is fully tested. also, we need clear SLA credit terms.
[10:53] Tom: procurement needs the SLA clause and a timeline for any credits or PRs.
[11:00] Rajiv: we'll start the SLA review parallel to the RCA.

[11:07] Sofia: open technical risk: model-routing compatibility check needs a small release; we expect patch in 2 weeks.
[11:14] Ethan: open operational risk: alert dedupe rule will be removed in 24-48 hours and replaced with finer-grained dedupe.

[11:22] Dana: question — is this incident likely to recur with other customers?
[11:27] Ethan: the root cause is a workload-specific interaction (long sequences + retry storm) but the mitigations will be rolled out globally because it touches the runtime and autoscaler. so other customers would have been at risk.

[11:40] Liam: can Redwood share a quick patch timeline so we can plan change windows?
[11:45] Sofia: yes: memory accounting patch is live on canary now, will go to blue/green next Wednesday (2025-03-12) pending staging replay. autoscaler tweaks to follow after internal stress tests, target 2025-03-19.

[12:05] Ethan: Ok — final recap: we'll share the annotated postmortem and logs by 2025-03-07; Sofia will publish the runbook and draft circuit breaker by 2025-03-12; Priya and Liam will exchange audit and config snapshots by 2025-03-10; Rajiv/Ethan schedule follow-up 2025-03-19.
[12:30] Dana: thanks all — appreciate the quick response and the detailed plan.
[12:35] Ethan: thanks everyone. we'll follow up in writing and post the document to the shared bucket.
[12:40] (meeting ends)

title: Int8 vs FP8 vs GPTQ vs AWQ — quant tradeoffs + on-prem deployment check-in
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-02-11
Start: 15:30 UTC
Duration: ~58 minutes
Location: Zoom
Recording: yes

Attendees: Alex (Redwood AE), Jordan (Redwood SE), Samira (Product), Ethan (SRE) — Priya (NimbleHealth ML), Marcus (Infra), Lena (Head AI)

Auto-summary (auto gen, may be rough):
- Deep dive on quantization options (INT8 / FP8 / GPTQ / AWQ) and how they map to accuracy/latency tradeoffs for NimbleHealth clinical prompts.
- Discussed KV cache interactions, batching, and on-prem VPC constraints.
- Agreed on next steps: share micro-benchmarks, customer to send prompt corpus, schedule repro session.

Topics: quantization accuracy regressions, calibration data, batch sizing, prefix caching, VPC KMS rules, fallback strategies.

Transcript body:
[00:00:05] Alex (Redwood AE): Hey everyone, thanks for joining — short agenda: Jordan will run through micro-benchmarks and tradeoffs, then we'll talk about your on-prem constraints and next steps.
[00:00:16] Priya (NimbleHealth): Thanks, Alex. We mainly care about latency at p95 for clinical note generation and zero-tolerance for hallucinations on protected fields. We're trying to see if quant can save costs without regression.
[00:00:28] Jordan (Redwood SE): Yep that's the right framing. Quick callout — quantization isn't magic; it's a set of tradeoffs. INT8 gives big memory savings and often good perf, but you do see accuracy drift on sensitive tokens unless calibration is carefully done. GPTQ/AWQ are post-training quant methods more optimized for LLMs.
[00:00:44] Marcus (Infra): Quick Q — do we need FP8 for best perf on A100 vs T4? Our on-prem fleet is mixed.
[00:00:50] Jordan (SE): Short answer: FP8 can get you single-step speedups on certain hardware that has FP8 fast paths, but FP8 benefits depend on kernel support. For A100 gen3 maybe, but older T4s won't help. INT8 + AWQ/GPTQ often gives the best cross-hardware portability.
[00:01:05] Lena (Head AI): We ran an internal run with GPTQ and saw a small BLEU drop on our summarization set; some outputs were shorter. Any suggestions?
[00:01:15] Jordan (SE): That’s expected. GPTQ can change distribution subtly. We typically do three things: 1) calibrate on domain prompts (you sent a few examples earlier, which is great), 2) apply per-channel quantization where available, and 3) tune decoding temperature/penalty to match length. Also consider hybrid approach — keep key layers in FP16 or FP32.
[00:01:36] Priya: On the calibration point — how many samples do you need? We can't share PHI, so we'll scrub.
[00:01:42] Samira (Product): For useful calibration you generally want a few thousand tokens across prompt/response pairs; but even 200-500 targeted examples can catch big regressions. The team often starts with 500-1000 domain-specific samples.
[00:01:56] Priya: OK we'll aim to provide 1k scrubbed prompts.
[00:02:02] Jordan (SE): Great. Now about AWQ specifically — AWQ is optimized for transformer weight quant with attention-aware heuristics; in practice we've seen AWQ hold accuracy better than naive INT8 on many decoder-only models, but it's also more sensitive to outlier dimensions and can require a slightly different calibration flow.
[00:02:18] Marcus: Are there known pathological cases where AWQ makes things worse?
[00:02:22] Jordan: Rarely but yes — when models have heavy low-rank adapters or very sparse heads, AWQ's heuristics can mis-estimate scaling for some heads, leading to token-level regressions. That's why we recommend a quick regression test suite before promoting a quantized build.
[00:02:36] [00:02:36] (audio noise) Speaker 4: mm-hmm
[00:02:42] Priya: Can you walk through an example of a regression we should look for?
[00:02:46] Jordan: Sure — example: protected PHI fields being replaced or redacted incorrectly, or repetition/early-stopping for clinical bullet lists. On one customer case, GPTQ caused truncation in long templates because length-prediction tokens shifted slightly. The fix was hybridizing the last 4 transformer blocks to FP16.
[00:03:06] Alex: Another operational approach is to add targeted evals that check protected-field fidelity. Redwood's Optimize product can instrument prompt sets and watch for drift post-quant.
[00:03:15] Lena: Tell us about KV cache implications — we rely heavily on prefix caching to speed up common templates.
[00:03:20] Jordan: KV cache itself is orthogonal to weight quantization, but quant affects memory layout and KV cache eviction policies indirectly. For example, INT8 weights reduce model memory leaving more headroom for longer KV caches, which helps latency for repeated contexts. But watch out for alignment/padding — some quant kernels require different memory alignment that can actually increase per-token latency in tight loops if not tuned.
[00:03:45] Marcus: So batching — our throughput target is 200 rps during peak, p95 latency under 350ms. If we apply quant, can we meet that on our 4xA100 nodes?
[00:03:55] Jordan: With proper batching and KV cache warm-up, yes. Our rough numbers: FP16 baseline on 4xA100 for your model family gave ~150 rps with p95 ~420ms; INT8+AWQ tuning pushed that to ~230 rps p95 ~320ms. But your mileage will vary; that's why we want to run micro-benchmarks on your exact prompts.
[00:04:15] Priya: You mentioned micro-benchmarks earlier — what metrics should we capture?
[00:04:19] Jordan: Key metrics: p50/p95/p99 latency, tokens/sec throughput, memory used (model+kvcache), stall events (garbage collection/IO waits), and accuracy deltas on the prompt suite (exact-match, containment for PHI, semantic similarity). Also track tail latencies during batch packing.
[00:04:36] [00:04:36] Alex: We'll provide a perf script that logs these, plus a small self-serve dashboard.
[00:04:41] Marcus: For VPC/on-prem testing — we have strict egress rules and a locked-down KMS. How do we get Redwood to run a repro without giving keys?
[00:04:49] Ethan (SRE): For private deployments we can use a short-lived test token and have you run the perf scripts inside your VPC against a Redwood-agent image. Alternatively, we can provide a reproducible docker bundle that runs fully offline; the only requirement is we need a list of allowed ports and KMS handshake endpoints if you want us to validate encryption at rest.
[00:05:06] Priya: We prefer the docker bundle.
[00:05:08] Jordan: Okay — AWQ/GPTQ repro docker includes quant toolchain, a small sample model shard, and a harness to run your prompt suite. Note: we don't include customer data; you'll run the harness inside your network and then share the metrics artifact.
[00:05:21] Priya: Perfect.
[00:05:24] Lena: Are there licensing or export issues with GPTQ or AWQ implementations? We have legal constraints.
[00:05:30] Alex: Good point. AWQ toolchain is under a permissive license in our distro, GPTQ forks vary. We'll include a license summary in the repo we share. Also, Redwood's Private package supports air-gapped deployment under enterprise contract if needed.
[00:05:45] Marcus: Something we hit last time — after quant we had a few memory alignment faults in our TF serving stack because kernels expected 64-byte alignment. Will Redwood's runtime handle alignment automatically?
[00:05:55] Jordan: Our runtime includes kernel selection and memory padding logic but when using in-house kernel variants in on-prem builds we sometimes need to set environment flags (REDWOOD_ALIGN=64) or tune allocator. We'll document the flags in the runbook.
[00:06:09] [00:06:09] (brief crosstalk) Speaker 4: right right
[00:06:15] Priya: On accuracy we can't accept more than 0.5% drop on our key metrics — is that realistic with INT8?
[00:06:22] Jordan: With domain calibration and hybrid FP16 on sensitive layers, yes it's realistic for many models, but not guaranteed. Some models, particularly small fine-tuned instruction-tuned models, are more brittle. We'll measure with your scrubbed prompts and then recommend either GPTQ/AWQ or hybridization.
[00:06:39] Alex: Another operational guard is rollout policy — canary quant builds at low percent traffic, roll back automatically on regression signals. Redwood's Console supports canary + automatic fallback to FP16 model in less than a minute if an eval alert hits.
[00:06:54] Marcus: How invasive is hybridizing the last N layers? Do we need to retrain?
[00:06:59] Jordan: No retraining — hybrid means keep weight tensors for select layers in FP16/FP32 while quantizing others. That increases model size slightly but preserves critical distribution info. The tradeoff is per-token latency might go up a bit for those layers but often still net positive.
[00:07:14] Priya: On the topic of AWQ vs GPTQ — which is easier to debug when you see regressions?
[00:07:20] Jordan: GPTQ tends to be more deterministic in behaviour because it's a quantization algorithm tied closely to the per-channel statistics; AWQ adds heuristic attention-aware steps, which can make debugging trickier. But AWQ often gives better out-of-the-box accuracy.
[00:07:33] Lena: Any guidance on decoding hyperparams to counteract short outputs after quant?
[00:07:37] Jordan: Increase repetition penalty slightly, bump min-length, or adjust length-penalty and temperature. We usually snapshot decoder hyperparams as part of the quant rollout.
[00:07:46] [00:07:46] (typing sounds)
[00:07:49] Alex: Quick pragmatic checklist for NimbleHealth: 1) Provide 1k scrubbed prompts; 2) Redwood shares AWQ/GPTQ docker + perf script; 3) Nimble runs inside VPC and returns metrics + sample outputs; 4) joint call to review diffs and pick quant strategy; 5) schedule canary rollout.
[00:08:08] Priya: Sounds good.
[00:08:11] Marcus: A question on monitoring — if quantization causes a silent semantic drift, what signals should we set to catch it before customers see it?
[00:08:19] Samira (Product): We recommend a layered approach: 1) unit-level exact-match tests for templates, 2) semantic similarity scoring against golden responses (embedding drift), 3) token-level anomaly detectors (unexpected token distributions), and 4) user-facing quality metrics (NPS or error reports). Redwood's Optimize can automate the first three.
[00:08:38] Priya: For semantic drift thresholds, any rule-of-thumb?
[00:08:41] Jordan: Watch for >2% drop in semantic similarity or >0.5% drop in exact-match for critical templates. But thresholds depend on your risk tolerance — for PHI you want near-zero tolerance.
[00:08:52] [00:08:52] (brief pause)
[00:08:57] Marcus: Two operational concerns: disk I/O during quant conversion (we saw CPU spikes) and kernel compile times for on-prem. Any mitigation?
[00:09:04] Jordan: Convert jobs are CPU-heavy and I/O bound; run them off-peak and use local SSDs to speed up sharding. For kernel compiles, prebuilt kernel images can be supplied — Redwood will provide these for your target kernels to avoid long compile cycles.
[00:09:18] Lena: If we see a regression in the canary, what's the rollback runbook?
[00:09:22] Alex: Automated rollback via Console — canary traffic is small, and if the eval alert triggers, we immediately shift traffic to the FP16 variant and capture failing traces. Post-mortem includes differential token analysis and layer-level ablation tests.
[00:09:36] Priya: We need to make sure logs don't leak PHI in traces.
[00:09:40] Ethan (SRE): We'll redact traces and only capture hashes/embeddings for drift checks; full outputs stay inside your VPC unless you opt-in.
[00:09:48] [00:09:48] (keyboard clack)
[00:09:52] Alex: Ok wrapping with timings — we'll share the docker and perf script by 2025-02-13, Jordan will drop the runbook, Marcus will confirm infra readiness by 2025-02-18, and we'll reconvene after metric upload.
[00:10:05] Priya: Thanks — one last nit: are there specific tokenizer edge cases with quant? We had mis-tokenization in previous runs.
[00:10:12] Jordan: Tokenization is upstream and doesn't change with quantization, but if quantization leads to changed whitespace or special token handling in generated text, that could surface. We recommend freezing tokenizer versions and including a tokenization sanity check in the regression suite.
[00:10:27] Alex: Great. Anything else?
[00:10:30] Lena: No, thanks team.
[00:10:33] Alex: Thanks everyone — action items as noted. We'll follow up with the shared folder link.

[rough end-of-transcript notes]:
- Transcript auto-generated; some names or acronyms may be mis-transcribed (e.g., "K V cash" for KV cache).
- Syllables/filler included; occasional overlapping speech labeled generically.
- Links shared during call: https://redwood.example.com/awq-docker (shared repo placeholder), https://redwood.example.com/perf-script

End of meeting.

Also consider hybrid approach — keep key layers in FP16 or FP32.

[00:01:42] Samira (Product): For useful calibration you generally want a few thousand tokens across prompt/response pairs; but even 200-500 targeted examples can catch big regressions. The team often starts with 500-1000 domain-specific samples.
[00:01:56] Priya: OK we'll aim to provide 1k scrubbed prompts.
[00:02:02] Jordan (SE): Great. Now about AWQ specifically — AWQ is optimized for transformer weight quant with attention-aware heuristics; in practice we've seen AWQ hold accuracy better than naive INT8 on many decoder-only models, but it's also more sensitive to outlier dimensions and can require a slightly different calibration flow.
[00:02:18] Marcus: Are there known pathological cases where AWQ makes things worse?
[00:02:22] Jordan: Rarely but yes — when models have heavy low-rank adapters or very sparse heads, AWQ's heuristics can mis-estimate scaling for some heads, leading to token-level regressions. That's why we recommend a quick regression test suite before promoting a quantized build.

[00:03:20] Jordan: KV cache itself is orthogonal to weight quantization, but quant affects memory layout and KV cache eviction policies indirectly. For example, INT8 weights reduce model memory leaving more headroom for longer KV caches, which helps latency for repeated contexts. But watch out for alignment/padding — some quant kernels require different memory alignment that can actually increase per-token latency in tight loops if not tuned.

[00:05:21] Priya: Perfect.

[00:05:30] Alex: Good point. AWQ toolchain is under a permissive license in our distro, GPTQ forks vary. We'll include a license summary in the repo we share. Also, Redwood's Private package supports air-gapped deployment under enterprise contract if needed.
[00:05:45] Marcus: Something we hit last time — after quant we had a few memory alignment faults in our TF serving stack because kernels expected 64-byte alignment. Will Redwood's runtime handle alignment automatically?

[00:07:20] Jordan: GPTQ tends to be more deterministic in behaviour because it's a quantization algorithm tied closely to the per-channel statistics; AWQ adds heuristic attention-aware steps, which can make debugging trickier. But AWQ often gives better out-of-the-box accuracy.

[00:10:30] Lena: No, thanks team.

title: Dedicated upgrade safety – staged rollouts / gates / rollback semantics (technical deep dive)

title: POC hosting rundown - WillowCare pilot criteria
preview:
MEETING HEADER
Date: 2025-01-14
Time: 11:00 AM PST
Duration: 58 minutes
Attendees: Ethan Park (Redwood AE), Sofia Alvarez (Redwood SE), Dr. Aisha Khan (WillowCare Head of ML), Marcus Lee (CTO), Rita Patel (Platform Engineer), Samir Gupta (Security)

SUMMARY: Short scoping to align on POC success criteria (latency SLOs, throughput), sizing and benchmark plan comparing Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private, data residency/SOC2 questions, and timeline for a two-week pilot. Agreed next steps and owners.

TOPICS: success criteria; workload sizing; p95 latency vs cost; bench plan; security; timeline and pilot cutover.

TRANSCRIPT:
[00:00] Ethan: Hey everyone, thanks for joining, I'm Ethan from Redwood, AE on the account. Quick roll call?
[00:07] Aisha: Aisha here, head of ML at WillowCare.
[00:10] Marcus: Marcus, CTO.
[00:12] Rita: Rita, platform.
[00:14] Samir: Samir, security.
[00:16] Sofia: Sofia, Redwood SE, hi all.

[01:05] Ethan: Cool. High level — the reason for the call is to scope the POC: what success looks like, what benchmarks you want us to run, and to pick a hosting mode for the pilot — hosted API, dedicated reserved capacity, or private (VPC/on-prem). We're expecting this to be a two-week technical pilot once we sign off on the pilot plan.
[01:25] Aisha: Yep that's right. From our side the primary use case is clinical-note summarization and triage prioritization. Key stakeholders are product and compliance, we're sensitive to PHI and run in AWS us‑east‑1. So data residency and audit logs matter.
[01:40] Marcus: Also we need predictable latency for triage — p95 under 300ms ideally for short notes (~150 tokens) for the UX path, and with bursts up to 200 qps during spikes. Cost is a concern but second to reliability.
[01:55] Sofia: Good, that helps. Quick clarification — when you say p95 300ms for 150 tokens that's end-to-end user facing latency including our routing and network, right?
[02:04] Marcus: Correct. From app to model response and back to user.

[02:40] Ethan: Right, so this drives whether Hosted is sufficient or Dedicated/Private is needed. Hosted usually achieves lower time-to-value and is fine for 95% of workloads, but for guaranteed p95 SLOs at spikes, Dedicated or Private with reserved capacity is typical. We'll capture concrete numbers in the bench.
[03:02] Aisha: If we go Dedicated, what granularity of reserved capacity — GPU counts, and do you have autoscale for bursts?
[03:12] Sofia: Yes, Dedicated is a reserved GPU pool. We size by tokens/sec and concurrency. Autoscale can be configured with burst capacity but there is a warm-up for extra nodes — so we recommend configuring a small burst band or pre-warming around expected traffic windows. We'll propose a sizing model after we see sample traffic.

[03:50] Rita: We are also evaluating Private VPC because our compliance team prefers customer-managed networking and KMS. Is on‑prem necessary? We're mostly fine with VPC endpoints, but if we need air‑gapped that's heavier.
[04:05] Ethan: Understood. Private VPC gives you the control and KMS/HSM integration and audit logs. On‑prem is an option but it's a larger project. For the pilot we can do a VPC-connected Private deployment if you can open peering/privatelink — otherwise Hosted may be acceptable with encryption and SOC2 but less network control.

[04:40] Samir: We need to see SOC2 report, key rotation details, and an audit log sample. Also data retention policy — do you persist inputs/outputs in Hosted?
[04:52] Sofia: Good call. For Hosted we have retention defaults but can disable retention for enterprise customers; Dedicated and Private give you full control. We'll attach our SOC2 and a sample audit log. Note: sometimes filenames or acronyms transcribe weirdly, e.g., our 'KV cache' notes might show up as 'key view cache' in exports, so apologize in advance for transcript noise.

[05:30] Ethan: For the benchmark plan we propose a 48-hour run that exercises three modes: Hosted (our standard production tier), Dedicated (reserved capacity sized to match expected qps), and a simulated Private (VPC with same instance types) — measure p50/p95/p99 latency, tokens/sec, steady-state throughput and cold-start behavior. Sofia will run this and share results.
[05:55] Aisha: For data we'll provide 5000 synthetic notes and a set of 200 critical 'edge' prompts. We want pass/fail thresholds: p95 < 300ms for the short path, p95 < 800ms for long summaries (~600 tokens), and throughput sustaining 150 qps with spikes to 200 qps with < 10% error rate.

[06:30] Sofia: Sounds reasonable. We'll instrument with tracing and show breakdowns: model compute time, network, and our routing overhead. We also include token-level cost estimates — that's helpful for your finance team.
[06:45] Marcus: Cost estimates are important. Can you show a delta between Hosted per-token and Dedicated amortized GPU cost for our expected burn rate?
[06:54] Ethan: Yes, Sofia will provide a cost model: per-token hosted vs amortized GPU cost with utilization assumptions (we'll include a 60–80% utilization band). Also quantization suggestions — smaller models on Dedicated can be cheaper while meeting quality.

[07:20] Aisha: Quality — we also need regression checks. If you recommend quantized 4-bit models, can we run a small eval set to confirm no drop in clinical accuracy?
[07:30] Sofia: Absolutely. We'll run the evaluation harness on your supplied prompts and report metrics (precision/recall-ish metrics for triage labels plus ROUGE for summarization). We'll flag any regressions.

[08:02] Rita: Network-wise, for VPC we'd want private endpoints. How long to set up Private VPC pilot? Our infra team usually needs a week to grant peering/logs.
[08:13] Ethan: If you can provision peering and shared subnets within 5 business days, we can do VPC pilot same week. On‑prem requires longer (multi‑week) and an operations checklist.

[08:40] Samir: On encryption, KMS integration is a must. Shared keys or customer keys only? Also SSO/SAML for access?
[08:50] Sofia: We support customer-managed keys via KMS/HSM in Private and Dedicated; Hosted supports BYOK for enterprise if needed. SAML SSO is supported in the Console for RBAC.

[09:10] Ethan: To recap success criteria proposed by WillowCare: (1) p95 < 300ms for short notes, (2) p95 < 800ms for long summaries, (3) <10% error rate at spikes up to 200 qps, (4) no regression on the evaluation set, (5) compliance checklist satisfied (SOC2, KMS, audit logs). Are we missing anything?
[09:30] Aisha: Also include sustained throughput test for at least 1 hour at target qps to validate thermal/perf stability.
[09:39] Marcus: And a rough cost projection for 6 months at expected burn.

[10:00] Sofia: We'll add a 1-hour soak test at target qps in the bench run.
[10:05] Ethan: OK, next on timeline. We're proposing bench results in one week (Sofia), security packet and SOC2 by Ethan in three business days, and pilot kickoff the week of 1/27 if all good.
[10:18] Aisha: That timeline works but product wants to see a demo before pilot kick; can we schedule a short demo of the Console and rollout features?
[10:25] Ethan: Yes, we can do a 30-minute demo. How about next Tuesday?
[10:28] Marcus: That works.

[10:45] Sofia: Quick note on Dedicated sizing methodology — we estimate required GPU based on tokens/sec * average tokens per request * model throughput (tokens/s per GPU). Example: at 150 qps with 150 tokens avg that's 22.5k tokens/s; if a single A100 can do ~150k tokens/s for the target model with batching, you'd need the corresponding fraction of a GPU, so we choose a configuration that avoids high tail latencies. We'll show a sizing spreadsheet.
[11:10] Rita: The math helps. Also please include fallback strategy — if Dedicated capacity spikes, can requests fall back to Hosted or lower-cost variant?
[11:20] Sofia: Yes, smart routing supports fallback to compatible model variants or Hosted fallback. We'll include policy examples for canary A/B and automatic fallback rules.

[11:48] Aisha: One more thing — our legal team asks about data deletion SLA for Hosted. Do you have a documented process?
[11:56] Ethan: We do; we'll include a deletion SLA and process map in the security packet.

[12:10] Samir: Any competitors mentioned in our evaluation? We're also comparing Anthropic's endpoints and OpenAI, primarily for latency and cost. We may ask for a side-by-side latency/cost note.
[12:20] Sofia: We'll include competitor context at a high level but won't do direct benchmarking without permission.

[12:37] Marcus: Final recap and owners?
[12:40] Ethan: Sure. Action items — I'll send SOC2 and network diagram; Sofia runs the 48-hour bench and 1-hour soak across Hosted, Dedicated and VPC; Aisha sends prompt set; Marcus and Rita coordinate VPC peering; Samir reviews security docs. We'll aim to review bench results and demo next week and, if green, kick pilot week of 1/27.

[13:10] Aisha: Sounds good. Minor point — in previous transcripts some names were butchered, so apologies if that shows up.
[13:17] Ethan: No worries. Thanks everyone, we'll follow up with an email summarizing next steps.

[13:30] (Meeting ends)

NOTES AND LINKS: Security packet (link to internal doc), sample benchmark spreadsheet, Console demo recording (to be scheduled).

TRANSCRIPTION NOTES: Some words transcribed imperfectly (e.g., 'KV cache' -> 'key view cache'), slight speaker overlap around 06:45-07:30.

[11:10] Rita: The math helps. Also please include fallback strategy — if Dedicated capacity spikes, can requests fall back to Hosted or lower-cost variant?
[11:20] Sofia: Yes, smart routing supports fallback to compatible model variants or Hosted fallback. We'll include policy examples for canary A/B and automatic fallback rules.

[11:48] Aisha: One more thing — our legal team asks about data deletion SLA for Hosted. Do you have a documented process?
[11:56] Ethan: We do; we'll include a deletion SLA and process map in the security packet.

[12:10] Samir: Any competitors mentioned in our evaluation? We're also comparing Anthropic's endpoints and OpenAI, primarily for latency and cost. We may ask for a side-by-side latency/cost note.
[12:20] Sofia: We'll include competitor context at a high level but won't do direct benchmarking without permission.

[12:37] Marcus: Final recap and owners?
[12:40] Ethan: Sure. Action items — I'll send SOC2 and network diagram; Sofia runs the 48-hour bench and 1-hour soak across Hosted, Dedicated and VPC; Aisha sends prompt set; Marcus and Rita coordinate VPC peering; Samir reviews security docs. We'll aim to review bench results and demo next week and, if green, kick pilot week of 1/27.

[13:10] Aisha: Sounds good. Minor point — in previous transcripts some names were butchered, so apologies if that shows up.
[13:17] Ethan: No worries. Thanks everyone, we'll follow up with an email summarizing next steps.

[13:30] (Meeting ends)

NOTES AND LINKS: Security packet (link to internal doc), sample benchmark spreadsheet, Console demo recording (to be scheduled).

TRANSCRIPTION NOTES: Some words transcribed imperfectly (e.g., 'KV cache' -> 'key view cache'), slight speaker overlap around 06:45-07:30.
